name: Backyard Biodiversity Survey
description: Take a walk in your backyard, schoolyard, or park and observe all the living things you can find. Look for plants, insects, birds, spiders, worms, mushrooms, and microorganisms. Use a magnifying glass to examine small creatures. Make a list or drawing of each organism you find. Guess how the organisms depend on each other. What eats what? What provides shelter? Come back on different days or seasons and see how the community changes.
learning_objectives: Observe biodiversity in a real ecosystem
Identify different types of organisms (plants, insects, animals, fungi)
Begin to recognize species interactions and habitat structure
Record observations systematically
variations: Younger learners: focus on visible animals and plants, use pictures to identify
Advanced: use field guides to identify species and research their ecology
Seasonal: repeat survey each season and compare communities

name: Food Web Card Game
description: Create cards for different organisms in an ecosystem: plants, herbivores, carnivores, decomposers. Write energy on one side and arrows showing 'eats' or 'is eaten by' on the other. Play as a game: each organism card has a player. Energy flows from sun to plant to herbivore to carnivore. When a predator 'eats' a prey, energy passes. Players discover that removing one species affects many others. Expand to show multiple food chains connecting into a web.
materials: String or yarn to connect organisms
learning_objectives: Understand food chains and energy flow
Build multi-species food webs
Predict effects of removing one organism
Recognize interconnections in ecosystems
variations: Use a different local ecosystem (pond, forest, ocean)
Add decomposers and nutrient cycling
Quantify: herbivores eat 10% of plant energy, carnivores eat 10% of herbivore energy (10% rule)

name: Population Dynamics Simulation
description: Simulate predator-prey dynamics using a game. Rabbits (players with cards) hop around a field (classroom). Foxes (other players) try to tag rabbits. When a rabbit is tagged, it's eaten. Rabbits that survive the round breed (split a token into two). Foxes that didn't eat starve and are removed. Run multiple rounds and graph population changes. Discover cycles: more rabbits → more foxes → fewer rabbits → fewer foxes → more rabbits again.
materials: Playing cards or tokens for rabbits and foxes
learning_objectives: Understand predator-prey population cycles
Recognize that populations are dynamic and interconnected
Interpret population graphs
Predict consequences of removing predators or prey
variations: Add disease: some animals get sick and recover or die
Add environmental stress: periodic 'droughts' reduce food
Use dice or random events instead of physical chasing

name: Water Cycle in a Bag
description: Fill a ziplock bag with water and a plant cutting (or just water). Draw the water cycle on the bag: sun, evaporation arrows, condensation arrows, precipitation. Seal the bag. Put it in a sunny window. Over days, water evaporates from the soil and plant, rises in the bag, condenses on the plastic (clouds), drips back down (precipitation), and soaks into soil again. Watch the complete water cycle happen in miniature without any outside water being added.
materials: Soil and small plant or just water
learning_objectives: Understand evaporation, condensation, and precipitation
Observe the water cycle in action
Recognize that water is recycled, not consumed
Connect atmospheric water to ground water
variations: Add food coloring to track water movement
Compare sealed vs. open bags to show water loss
Create a larger terrarium water cycle

name: Carbon Cycle Diagram Builder
description: Draw or build a large carbon cycle diagram showing: atmosphere (CO₂), plants (photosynthesis captures carbon), animals (eating and respiration release CO₂), decomposers (breaking down dead things, releasing CO₂), fossil fuels (ancient plants and animals), and human activities (burning fossil fuels, releasing CO₂). Use arrows to show carbon moving between reservoirs. Add numbers to show rough timescales: photosynthesis happens in hours, decomposition in months to years, fossilization in millions of years.
materials: Pictures of ecosystems and fossil fuels
learning_objectives: Trace carbon through multiple reservoirs
Understand photosynthesis and respiration as inverse processes
Recognize that fossil fuel burning releases stored carbon
Understand timescales of different processes
Predict consequences of fossil fuel burning
variations: Compare pre-industrial vs. modern carbon cycle (industrial carbon inputs)
Quantify: add approximate gigatons of carbon per year
Extend to ocean carbon (dissolved CO₂, shells, deep sediments)

name: Modeling Atmospheric CO2 Increase
description: Use real data to build a graph of atmospheric CO₂ from 1960 to today. Notice the sawtooth pattern (seasonal cycles as plants grow and decay) and the upward trend (human carbon). Calculate the rate of increase: ~2 ppm per year. Extend the line forward: if the trend continues, what will CO₂ be in 2050? Discuss factors that could change the trend (renewable energy, reforestation). Contrast with ice core data showing CO₂ naturally varied 180-280 ppm over 800,000 years; current increase is 10x faster.
learning_objectives: Interpret real atmospheric CO₂ data
Recognize trends in noisy data
Make predictions based on trends
Understand that current change is rapid compared to natural variability
Understand the basis for climate projections
variations: Compare CO₂ to other greenhouse gases (methane, nitrous oxide)
Relate CO₂ increase to human activities (fossil fuel burning, deforestation)
Use climate model projections: graph future temperatures under different CO₂ scenarios

name: Trash Audit: What Does Your Family Throw Away?
description: Over one week, collect your family's trash (with permission). Sort each item by category: food, paper, plastic, metal, glass, electronics, etc. Weigh or count items. Create a bar graph showing what makes up most waste. Investigate where items end up: landfill, recycling, compost. Research how long each item takes to decompose. Propose ways to reduce the largest waste categories. Calculate how much waste your family generates per year; scale to your community.
materials: Field guide to waste decomposition times
learning_objectives: Understand composition of household waste
Recognize that 'throwing away' doesn't make trash disappear
Understand decomposition timescales and environmental impacts
Propose reduction, reuse, and recycling strategies
Connect individual behavior to community and environmental impacts
variations: Audit school waste instead of family waste
Research what fraction is recyclable vs. landfill vs. compost
Calculate cost of waste management in your community

name: Water Quality Testing
description: Test water from a local stream, pond, or tap for indicators of quality. Use pH paper to measure acidity. Use turbidity tubes to measure cloudiness (suspended solids). Count macroinvertebrates (aquatic insects) which indicate healthy conditions. Test for nutrients (nitrogen/phosphorus) that cause algae blooms. Record data on a map: pollution correlates with distance from potential sources (factories, farms, sewers). Propose explanations for pollution patterns.
learning_objectives: Understand water quality indicators
Design a simple scientific investigation
Collect and analyze data
Connect land use to water quality
Recognize sources of pollution
Propose water protection strategies
variations: Test multiple sites and compare quality
Monitor the same site over weeks to see changes
Research pollutants in your watershed

name: Greenhouse Effect in a Jar Demonstration
description: Set up two jars in sunlight: one open, one sealed with plastic wrap. Place a thermometer in each. The sealed jar heats up more because trapped air (like greenhouse gases) prevents heat from escaping. Add CO₂ to the sealed jar (breath, or leavened baking soda + vinegar) and it heats even more. This mimics how greenhouse gases trap heat in the atmosphere. Measure temperatures over 15 minutes and graph. Discuss: this is the principle behind climate change, but the atmosphere is much more complex.
materials: Dry ice or baking soda + vinegar (optional for added CO₂)
learning_objectives: Understand the greenhouse effect mechanism
Recognize that CO₂ and other gases trap heat
Perform a controlled experiment with variables
Interpret data to support claims
Understand a simplified model of climate dynamics
variations: Compare different gases (CO₂, methane, air) in sealed jars
Vary jar size or insulation and compare
Model feedback: ice reflects light (lower temperatures) vs. dark ground absorbs heat (higher temperatures)

name: Climate Data Graphing Challenge
description: Download real climate data: global temperature anomalies, CO₂ levels, sea level rise, ice extent, or sea surface temperature. Graph the data over the past 100+ years. Identify patterns: warming trend, acceleration, variability. Compare multiple datasets and look for correlations. Create a presentation explaining what the data shows, what uncertainties exist, and what it predicts about the future.
learning_objectives: Read and interpret large real datasets
Distinguish correlation from causation
Understand confidence intervals and error bars
Recognize patterns in noisy data
Communicate findings visually
Understand the strength of multiple independent datasets pointing to the same conclusion
variations: Compare regional data to global trends
Project future trends (with uncertainty ranges)
Evaluate contrarian claims against the data

name: Energy Audit of Your Home
description: Conduct an energy audit of your home: identify where energy is used (heating/cooling, water heating, appliances, lighting, transportation). Estimate energy consumption of major systems (look at utility bills). Calculate carbon footprint using online calculators. Identify the biggest energy users. Research efficiency improvements: insulation, LED lighting, efficient appliances, renewable energy options, transportation alternatives. Estimate cost and energy savings from each. Propose a prioritized plan for reducing energy use.
learning_objectives: Understand personal energy consumption patterns
Calculate carbon footprints
Identify energy waste and efficiency opportunities
Evaluate tradeoffs between cost and efficiency
Understand energy transition and renewable alternatives
Connect personal choices to community and global impacts
variations: Audit your school instead of home
Compare energy use across homes of different sizes
Calculate payback period for efficiency upgrades

name: Design a Sustainable Community
description: Design a sustainable community for 10,000 people. Plan for: energy (renewable sources), food (local agriculture), water (conservation and treatment), transportation (walkability, transit, electric vehicles), waste (reduction and recycling), housing (efficient construction), and ecosystem protection (green space, habitat corridors). Use maps and diagrams. Estimate energy, water, and food needs. Identify constraints and tradeoffs: density vs. green space, renewable energy vs. cost, local agriculture vs. food variety. Present your design with justifications for major decisions.
materials: Graph paper or digital design tools
Maps or aerial photos of a real location
Sustainability data (energy requirements, water use, etc.)
learning_objectives: Apply systems thinking to design real-world sustainability
Understand resource flows and constraints
Recognize and articulate tradeoffs
Consider multiple dimensions of sustainability (environmental, social, economic)
Communicate complex design decisions
Appreciate that sustainable solutions require integration across domains
variations: Design a sustainable school building or campus
Redesign your actual community for sustainability
Compare designs and defend design choices
